steps.prompt: Which is the main finite verb?

hints.bibendum: 'bibendum' is a gerundive, not a finite verb
hints.libero: 'libero' is an adjective meaning 'free' in the ablative

hints.to eat: 'to eat' is 'edere' — 'est' here comes from 'esse' (to be)

hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the principal parts of 'stare' (to stand)
hints.edo, edere, edi, esum: These are the principal parts of 'edere' (to eat)
hints.eo, ire, ivi, itum: These are the principal parts of 'ire' (to go)

hints.Gerund: A gerund is a verbal noun — 'bibendum' here with 'est' forms a passive periphrastic (gerundive)
hints.Supine: Supine would be 'bibitum' — 'bibendum' with -nd- is a gerundive
hints.Perfect participle: Perfect participle would be 'bibitus' — 'bibendum' has the gerundive -nd- marker

steps.prompt: What mood does the gerundive express?

reveal.gloss: that must be drunk / one must drink

hints.Purpose: Purpose uses 'ut' + subjunctive — the gerundive + 'est' expresses obligation
hints.Result: Result uses 'ut' + subjunctive — this is a passive periphrastic expressing necessity
hints.Condition: Conditions use 'si' — the gerundive with 'est' expresses obligation

hints.vivo, vivere, vixi, victum: These are the principal parts of 'vivere' (to live)
hints.scribo, scribere, scripsi, scriptum: These are the principal parts of 'scribere' (to write)
hints.dico, dicere, dixi, dictum: These are the principal parts of 'dicere' (to speak)

hints.Nominative: Nominative of 'pes' is 'pes' — 'pede' with -e ending is ablative

hints.1st: 1st declension nouns end in -a — 'pes' with genitive 'pedis' is 3rd declension
hints.2nd: 2nd declension nouns end in -us/-um — 'pes' is 3rd declension
hints.4th: 4th declension nouns have genitive -us — 'pes' has genitive 'pedis'

translation: Now one must drink, now with a free foot
